foundations_of_inference.definition: The branch of statistics concerned with drawing conclusions about population parameters from sample data, quantifying uncertainty in those conclusions.
foundations_of_inference.intuition: Statistical inference is the formal machinery for learning from data under uncertainty — turning observations into principled statements about the world.
foundations_of_inference.example_use: Estimating the population mean income from a survey sample and constructing a confidence interval around that estimate.

frequentist_inference.definition: A framework for statistical inference that interprets probability as long-run frequency and evaluates procedures by their repeated-sampling behavior.
frequentist_inference.intuition: Frequentist methods ask: if we repeated this experiment many times, how would our procedure perform on average?
frequentist_inference.common_confusion: Frequentist confidence intervals do not mean 'the parameter is in this interval with 95% probability' — the parameter is fixed, not random.
frequentist_inference.example_use: A 95% confidence interval procedure covers the true parameter in 95% of repeated samples.

parameter.definition: A numerical characteristic of a population or probability model, typically unknown and to be estimated from data.
parameter.intuition: Parameters are the unknowns that define a model — estimating them is the central task of statistical inference.
parameter.common_confusion: Parameters describe populations; statistics describe samples. The sample mean is a statistic that estimates the population mean parameter.
parameter.example_use: The mean μ and variance σ² of a normal distribution are its parameters.

parameter_space.definition: The set of all possible values a parameter can take, defining the scope of the statistical model.
parameter_space.intuition: The parameter space constrains what models are considered — for example, a variance must be positive, so σ² ∈ (0, ∞).
parameter_space.example_use: For a Binomial model, the parameter space is p ∈ (0, 1).

continuous_parameter.definition: A parameter that can take any value in a continuous range, such as a mean, variance, or regression coefficient.
continuous_parameter.intuition: Most statistical parameters are continuous — they live on the real line or a continuous subset of it.
continuous_parameter.example_use: The regression slope β ∈ ℝ is a continuous parameter.

discrete_parameter.definition: A parameter that can only take values from a discrete set, such as the number of mixture components or the degrees of freedom.
discrete_parameter.intuition: Discrete parameters arise when the model structure itself is unknown — selecting the number of clusters is a discrete parameter problem.
discrete_parameter.example_use: The number of components k in a Gaussian mixture model is a discrete parameter.

population_mean.definition: The average of a numerical variable across all members of a population, denoted μ.
population_mean.intuition: The population mean is the true center of the distribution — what the sample mean tries to estimate.
population_mean.common_confusion: The population mean is a fixed unknown constant. The sample mean X̄ is a random variable that estimates it.
population_mean.example_use: The mean height of all adults in a country is a population parameter estimated from a sample survey.

population_variance.definition: The average squared deviation of a variable from its population mean, measuring the spread of the entire population.
population_variance.intuition: Population variance quantifies how spread out the true distribution is — the sample variance S² estimates it.
population_variance.common_confusion: The sample variance divides by n-1 (not n) to be an unbiased estimator of σ².
population_variance.example_use: The variance of exam scores across all students in a school is a population parameter.

population_proportion.definition: The fraction of a population with a particular characteristic, denoted p.
population_proportion.intuition: Population proportion is the true probability of a binary outcome in the population.
population_proportion.example_use: The proportion of voters supporting a candidate in an election is estimated from a poll.

independent_random_samples.definition: A sample of n observations drawn independently from the same population, forming the basis of most classical statistical methods.
independent_random_samples.intuition: Independent random sampling ensures each observation carries fresh, unrelated information about the population.
independent_random_samples.common_confusion: In practice, sampling without replacement from a finite population introduces weak dependence. If the population is large relative to the sample, this is negligible.
independent_random_samples.example_use: Drawing 100 voters at random from a voter registry yields an independent random sample.

empirical_distribution.definition: The distribution that places equal probability mass 1/n on each of the n observed data points, serving as a nonparametric estimate of the population distribution.
empirical_distribution.intuition: The empirical distribution is the simplest possible estimate of the true distribution — just treat the data as the population.
empirical_distribution.example_use: The bootstrap method samples from the empirical distribution to approximate sampling distributions.

sample_mean.definition: The arithmetic average of n observed values, X̄ = (1/n)Σ Xᵢ, used as an estimator of the population mean μ.
sample_mean.intuition: The sample mean is the most natural estimator of center — unbiased, consistent, and asymptotically normal by the CLT.
sample_mean.common_confusion: The sample mean is a random variable with its own distribution (the sampling distribution). It is not the same as μ.
sample_mean.example_use: The average of 50 measurements estimates the true mean of the measurement process.

sample_variance.definition: The average squared deviation from the sample mean, computed with divisor n-1 to produce an unbiased estimator of σ².
sample_variance.intuition: Dividing by n-1 instead of n corrects for the fact that deviations are measured from the estimated mean X̄, not the true mean μ.
sample_variance.common_confusion: Dividing by n gives the MLE of σ² (biased). Dividing by n-1 gives the unbiased estimator. The difference matters most for small n.
sample_variance.example_use: S² estimates the variability of measurements in an experiment.

sample_standard_deviation.definition: The square root of the sample variance, S = √S², providing a spread measure in the same units as the data.
sample_standard_deviation.intuition: Standard deviation is variance expressed in the original units — easier to interpret and compare to the mean.
sample_standard_deviation.common_confusion: S is not an unbiased estimator of σ even though S² is unbiased for σ². Jensen's inequality ensures E[S] < σ.
sample_standard_deviation.example_use: A standard deviation of 5 cm in height measurements tells us most values are within ~5 cm of the mean.

sample_covariance.definition: The sample analog of covariance, measuring the linear co-movement between two variables in a dataset.
sample_covariance.intuition: Sample covariance estimates how two variables tend to move together in the data.
sample_covariance.example_use: The sample covariance between height and weight is positive, indicating taller people tend to weigh more.

sample_proportion.definition: The fraction of sample observations with a particular characteristic, p̂ = k/n, used to estimate the population proportion p.
sample_proportion.intuition: The sample proportion is an unbiased, consistent estimator of the population proportion.
sample_proportion.example_use: If 42 of 100 surveyed voters support a candidate, p̂ = 0.42 estimates the true support level.

sampling_distributions.definition: The probability distribution of a statistic (like X̄ or S²) over all possible samples of size n from the population.
sampling_distributions.intuition: A sampling distribution describes how much a statistic would vary if we repeatedly drew new samples — it quantifies estimator uncertainty.
sampling_distributions.common_confusion: The sampling distribution is not the distribution of the data. It is the distribution of a summary statistic computed from the data.
sampling_distributions.example_use: The sampling distribution of X̄ is N(μ, σ²/n) when sampling from a normal population.

sampling_distribution.definition: The distribution of a specific statistic computed from repeated random samples of the same size from the same population.
sampling_distribution.intuition: If you drew thousands of samples and computed X̄ each time, the histogram of those X̄ values would approximate its sampling distribution.
sampling_distribution.example_use: The sampling distribution of the sample mean has standard error σ/√n.

standard_error.definition: The standard deviation of a statistic's sampling distribution, measuring the typical variability of the statistic across samples.
standard_error.formula: SE(X̄) = σ/√n  (estimated as S/√n when σ unknown)
standard_error.intuition: Standard error tells you how precisely the statistic estimates its target — smaller SE means more reliable estimates.
standard_error.common_confusion: Standard error is the standard deviation of the estimator, not of the data. Confusing the two leads to wrong confidence intervals.
standard_error.example_use: With n=100 and S=10, the standard error of X̄ is 10/√100 = 1.

bootstrapped_sampling_distribution.definition: An approximation to the sampling distribution of a statistic obtained by repeatedly resampling with replacement from the observed data.
bootstrapped_sampling_distribution.intuition: The bootstrap pretends the empirical distribution is the true population and simulates what sampling variability would look like.
bootstrapped_sampling_distribution.common_confusion: The bootstrap approximates the sampling distribution of a statistic, not the population distribution of the data.
bootstrapped_sampling_distribution.example_use: Bootstrap the median to get a confidence interval when the analytical sampling distribution is unknown.

large_sample_inference.definition: Inference methods that rely on asymptotic approximations (typically the CLT or MLE theory) that become valid as sample size grows.
large_sample_inference.intuition: Large-sample methods trade exact results for generality — they work for almost any distribution when n is large enough.
large_sample_inference.common_confusion: How large n needs to be depends on the problem. For heavy-tailed distributions, n may need to be very large for the CLT approximation to be adequate.
large_sample_inference.example_use: Wald confidence intervals for MLE estimates rely on large-sample normality of the MLE.

large_sample_distribution_of_the_mle.definition: Under regularity conditions, the MLE θ̂ is asymptotically normal: √n(θ̂ - θ₀) →_d N(0, I(θ₀)⁻¹) where I(θ₀) is the Fisher information.
large_sample_distribution_of_the_mle.intuition: MLEs are approximately normal for large samples — this justifies Wald confidence intervals and z-tests for any parametric model.
large_sample_distribution_of_the_mle.common_confusion: This is an asymptotic result. For small samples, the MLE may be far from normal, especially for skewed or bounded parameters.
large_sample_distribution_of_the_mle.example_use: The asymptotic normality of the logistic regression MLE justifies z-tests for regression coefficients.

normal_approximation.definition: The use of the normal distribution to approximate the distribution of a statistic, justified by the Central Limit Theorem.
normal_approximation.intuition: When n is large, the normal approximation converts difficult probability calculations into simple z-score lookups.
normal_approximation.common_confusion: The normal approximation can be poor for small samples, rare events, or heavily skewed distributions.
normal_approximation.example_use: Approximating the Binomial(n, p) distribution by N(np, np(1-p)) for large n.

point_estimation.definition: The use of a single value (a statistic) to estimate an unknown population parameter.
point_estimation.intuition: Point estimation boils down all the data to a single best guess for the unknown parameter.
point_estimation.common_confusion: A point estimate ignores uncertainty. Always pair a point estimate with a measure of precision (SE, CI).
point_estimation.example_use: The sample mean X̄ = 4.7 is a point estimate of the population mean μ.

estimator.definition: A function of the sample data used to estimate an unknown parameter; a rule that maps data to an estimate.
estimator.intuition: An estimator is a recipe — plug in data, get out an estimate. The estimator itself is a random variable with its own distribution.
estimator.common_confusion: An estimator is a function (random variable); an estimate is its realized value for a specific dataset.
estimator.example_use: T(X₁,...,Xₙ) = X̄ is an estimator of μ; T(3,5,4,6) = 4.5 is an estimate.

unbiased_estimator.definition: An estimator whose expected value equals the true parameter value: E[T(X)] = θ for all θ.
unbiased_estimator.intuition: An unbiased estimator is correct on average — it neither systematically overshoots nor undershoots the true value.
unbiased_estimator.common_confusion: Unbiasedness is a desirable but not sufficient property. A biased estimator with lower variance may have smaller mean squared error.
unbiased_estimator.example_use: The sample mean X̄ is an unbiased estimator of μ. The sample variance S² is unbiased for σ².

bias.definition: The systematic difference between the expected value of an estimator and the true parameter: Bias(T) = E[T(X)] - θ.
bias.intuition: Bias measures the estimator's tendency to consistently over- or under-estimate the true value.
bias.common_confusion: MSE = Variance + Bias². Reducing bias can increase variance (the bias-variance trade-off).
bias.example_use: The MLE of σ² divides by n (not n-1), giving a negative bias of -σ²/n.

consistency.definition: An estimator Tₙ is consistent if it converges in probability to the true parameter as n → ∞: Tₙ →_p θ.
consistency.intuition: Consistency means more data guarantees a better estimate — in the limit, you recover the truth exactly.
consistency.common_confusion: Consistency is a large-sample property. An estimator can be consistent but have large bias or variance for finite n.
consistency.example_use: The sample mean is consistent for μ by the Law of Large Numbers.

estimate.definition: The specific numerical value produced by applying an estimator to an observed dataset.
estimate.intuition: An estimate is the output of the estimation recipe applied to your actual data — a single number.
estimate.example_use: Applying the sample mean estimator to {3, 5, 4, 6} gives the estimate 4.5.

bias_corrected_estimate.definition: An estimate adjusted to remove the known bias of an estimator: T* = T - Bias(T).
bias_corrected_estimate.intuition: If you know your estimator is systematically off, subtract the bias to get a corrected value.
bias_corrected_estimate.common_confusion: Bias correction increases variance. The corrected estimator may have higher MSE despite being unbiased.
bias_corrected_estimate.example_use: The bootstrap bias-corrected estimate subtracts the estimated bootstrap bias from the original estimate.

interval_estimation.definition: Estimation that produces a range of plausible values for a parameter rather than a single point, quantifying uncertainty.
interval_estimation.intuition: Interval estimates acknowledge that a single number cannot fully capture parameter uncertainty — a range is more honest.
interval_estimation.example_use: Reporting a 95% confidence interval (3.2, 5.8) rather than just the point estimate 4.5.

confidence_interval.definition: An interval [L(X), U(X)] constructed from sample data such that the procedure covers the true parameter with probability 1-α across repeated samples.
confidence_interval.intuition: A 95% CI is a random interval that traps the true parameter in 95% of repeated experiments — not a 95% probability statement about this particular interval.
confidence_interval.common_confusion: A 95% CI does NOT mean 'there is a 95% probability the parameter is in this interval.' The parameter is fixed; the interval is random.
confidence_interval.example_use: A 95% CI for μ: X̄ ± 1.96 σ/√n when σ is known and the population is normal.

confidence_level.definition: The probability 1-α that the confidence interval procedure captures the true parameter value over repeated samples.
confidence_level.intuition: Higher confidence level means wider intervals — there is a direct trade-off between precision and reliability.
confidence_level.common_confusion: Confidence level is a property of the procedure, not of any single computed interval.
confidence_level.example_use: A 99% CI is wider than a 95% CI for the same data, reflecting higher guaranteed coverage.

large_sample_confidence_interval.definition: A confidence interval constructed using the normal approximation to the sampling distribution of the statistic, valid for large n.
parameters: z_{α/2}: critical value from N(0,1), e.g. 1.96 for 95% CI
large_sample_confidence_interval.intuition: When n is large, the CLT guarantees approximate normality, making z-based intervals valid for any distribution.
large_sample_confidence_interval.common_confusion: The large-sample CI is approximate, not exact. For small samples, use t-intervals or exact methods.
large_sample_confidence_interval.example_use: X̄ ± 1.96 S/√n is a large-sample 95% CI for μ.

t_confidence_interval.definition: A confidence interval for a normal population mean using the t-distribution when σ² is unknown, especially appropriate for small samples.
parameters: t_{α/2, n-1}: critical value from t-distribution with n-1 degrees of freedom
t_confidence_interval.intuition: The t-interval replaces the z critical value with a larger t critical value to account for the extra uncertainty from estimating σ.
t_confidence_interval.common_confusion: The t-CI assumes normality of the population. For large n, the t and z critical values are nearly identical.
t_confidence_interval.example_use: For n=15 and 95% confidence: X̄ ± 2.145 S/√15.

confidence_interval_for_difference_of_me.definition: A confidence interval for μ₁ - μ₂, the difference between two population means, constructed from two independent samples.
confidence_interval_for_difference_of_me.intuition: Comparing two groups requires an interval for their difference — if it excludes 0, the means are significantly different.
confidence_interval_for_difference_of_me.common_confusion: The pooled vs. unpooled standard error choice depends on whether equal variances can be assumed.
confidence_interval_for_difference_of_me.example_use: Constructing a 95% CI for the difference in mean blood pressure between a treatment and control group.

confidence_interval_for_population_varia.definition: A confidence interval for σ² based on the chi-square distribution of (n-1)S²/σ².
confidence_interval_for_population_varia.intuition: Unlike CI for the mean, the CI for variance is asymmetric because the chi-square distribution is right-skewed.
confidence_interval_for_population_varia.common_confusion: This interval assumes the population is exactly normal. It is not robust to departures from normality.
confidence_interval_for_population_varia.example_use: A 95% CI for the variance of a manufacturing process to check if it meets specifications.

large_sample_confidence_interval_using_m.definition: A confidence interval derived from the asymptotic normality of the MLE: θ̂ ± z_{α/2} / √(nI(θ̂)).
large_sample_confidence_interval_using_m.intuition: MLE theory provides standard errors for free — the Fisher information gives the asymptotic variance.
large_sample_confidence_interval_using_m.example_use: Confidence intervals for logistic regression coefficients use the MLE asymptotic normality result.

wald_confidence_interval.definition: A confidence interval of the form θ̂ ± z_{α/2} · SE(θ̂), based on the normal approximation to the distribution of the MLE.
wald_confidence_interval.intuition: The Wald interval is the simplest and most widely used form of CI — estimate plus or minus a multiple of its standard error.
wald_confidence_interval.common_confusion: Wald intervals can have poor coverage for proportions near 0 or 1, or for small samples. The Wilson interval performs better in those cases.
wald_confidence_interval.example_use: A Wald 95% CI for a proportion: p̂ ± 1.96 √(p̂(1-p̂)/n).

likelihood.definition: The likelihood of parameter θ given observed data x is L(θ; x) = f(x; θ), the data density evaluated at the observed data as a function of θ.
likelihood.formula: L(θ; x) = f(x; θ) = ∏ᵢ f(xᵢ; θ)  (IID case)
likelihood.intuition: The likelihood measures how probable the observed data would be under different parameter values — higher likelihood means the data is more consistent with that parameter.
likelihood.common_confusion: The likelihood is not a probability distribution over θ. It is a function of θ for fixed observed data x.
likelihood.example_use: For n Bernoulli observations with k successes: L(p; k) = p^k (1-p)^(n-k).

log_likelihood.definition: The natural logarithm of the likelihood function, ℓ(θ) = log L(θ; x), used because it converts products to sums and is easier to maximize.
log_likelihood.formula: ℓ(θ) = log L(θ; x) = Σᵢ log f(xᵢ; θ)
log_likelihood.intuition: Taking the log makes the math tractable — products become sums, and the logarithm is monotone so it doesn't change where the maximum is.
log_likelihood.example_use: For normal data: ℓ(μ, σ²) = -n/2 log(2πσ²) - Σ(xᵢ-μ)²/(2σ²).

maximum_likelihood_estimation.definition: A method of estimating parameters by finding the value θ̂ that maximizes the likelihood (or equivalently the log-likelihood) of the observed data.
maximum_likelihood_estimation.formula: θ̂_MLE = argmax_θ L(θ; x) = argmax_θ ℓ(θ; x)
maximum_likelihood_estimation.intuition: MLE finds the parameter value that makes the observed data most probable — the best explanation for what was seen.
maximum_likelihood_estimation.common_confusion: MLE maximizes the likelihood of the data given θ, not the probability of θ given the data. That is Bayesian posterior maximization.
maximum_likelihood_estimation.example_use: The MLE of μ for normal data is X̄; the MLE of p for Binomial data is k/n.

maximum_likelihood_estimator.definition: The specific estimator θ̂ that results from applying maximum likelihood estimation to a given parametric model.
maximum_likelihood_estimator.formula: θ̂ = argmax_θ Σᵢ log f(Xᵢ; θ)
maximum_likelihood_estimator.intuition: The MLE is the gold standard for parametric estimation — it is consistent, asymptotically efficient, and invariant under reparameterization.
maximum_likelihood_estimator.common_confusion: The MLE is not always unbiased. For example, the MLE of σ² divides by n, not n-1, giving a biased (but consistent) estimator.
maximum_likelihood_estimator.example_use: In logistic regression, the MLE of β is found by iterative numerical optimization.

regularity_conditions.definition: Technical conditions on the parametric model (differentiability, identifiability, bounded support, etc.) required for MLE asymptotic theory to hold.
regularity_conditions.intuition: Regularity conditions are the fine print of MLE theory — they ensure the log-likelihood is well-behaved enough for asymptotic normality to apply.
regularity_conditions.common_confusion: Common violations include: uniform distribution (support depends on parameter), or a model with non-differentiable likelihood.
regularity_conditions.example_use: The Uniform(0, θ) model violates regularity conditions — its MLE θ̂ = X_(n) is not asymptotically normal.

numerical_maximization_methods.definition: Iterative algorithms for finding the maximum of the log-likelihood when a closed-form solution does not exist.
numerical_maximization_methods.intuition: Most likelihoods in real models cannot be maximized analytically — numerical methods climb the log-likelihood surface iteratively.
numerical_maximization_methods.example_use: Logistic regression, mixed models, and neural networks all require numerical optimization to find the MLE.

newton_raphson_method.definition: An iterative algorithm for finding the maximum of the log-likelihood using first and second derivatives: θ_{t+1} = θ_t - [ℓ''(θ_t)]⁻¹ ℓ'(θ_t).
newton_raphson_method.intuition: Newton-Raphson uses local curvature information to take smarter steps toward the maximum than simple gradient ascent.
newton_raphson_method.common_confusion: Newton-Raphson can diverge or converge to a local maximum. It requires the log-likelihood to be concave near the solution.
newton_raphson_method.example_use: Used in IRLS (Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares) to fit GLMs including logistic regression.

bisection_method.definition: A root-finding algorithm that repeatedly halves an interval until the score function (likelihood derivative) equals zero.
bisection_method.intuition: Bisection is slower than Newton-Raphson but guaranteed to converge for one-dimensional problems with a sign change.
bisection_method.example_use: Finding the MLE of an exponential family parameter when Newton-Raphson is numerically unstable.

fisher_information.definition: A measure of the amount of information that observable data carries about an unknown parameter, equal to the variance of the score function.
fisher_information.formula: I(θ) = E[(∂/∂θ log f(X;θ))²] = -E[∂²/∂θ² log f(X;θ)]
fisher_information.intuition: High Fisher information means the data is very informative about θ — the likelihood is sharply peaked around the true value.
fisher_information.common_confusion: Fisher information is about the data's informativeness, not the parameter's uncertainty. These are related but distinct concepts.
fisher_information.example_use: The asymptotic variance of the MLE is 1/(nI(θ)) — more information means a more precise estimate.

expected_fisher_information.definition: The Fisher information computed as the expected value of the squared score, taken over the data distribution.
expected_fisher_information.intuition: Expected Fisher information averages over all possible datasets — it is a property of the model at a given θ, not of the observed data.
expected_fisher_information.example_use: The expected Fisher information for a Bernoulli(p) model is 1/(p(1-p)).

observed_fisher_information.definition: The Fisher information evaluated at the MLE using the observed data: J(θ̂) = -ℓ''(θ̂), the negative curvature of the log-likelihood at the maximum.
observed_fisher_information.intuition: Observed Fisher information uses the actual data rather than expectations — it gives the curvature of the likelihood at the MLE.
observed_fisher_information.common_confusion: Expected and observed Fisher information agree asymptotically but differ in finite samples. Observed information is more commonly used for standard errors.
observed_fisher_information.example_use: The standard error of the MLE is approximately 1/√J(θ̂).

score_function.definition: The derivative of the log-likelihood with respect to θ, equal to zero at the MLE: s(θ) = ∂ℓ(θ)/∂θ.
score_function.formula: s(θ) = ∂/∂θ log L(θ; x) = Σᵢ ∂/∂θ log f(xᵢ; θ)
score_function.intuition: The score function measures how much the log-likelihood increases as you move the parameter — it is zero at the maximum.
score_function.common_confusion: The score has mean zero under the true parameter: E_θ[s(θ)] = 0. This is a key identity used in deriving MLE properties.
score_function.example_use: Setting the score to zero and solving gives the MLE estimating equations.

likelihood_ratio.definition: The ratio of the likelihood under two different parameter values, used to compare models and construct test statistics.
likelihood_ratio.intuition: A likelihood ratio near 1 means both parameter values explain the data equally well; a small ratio means θ₀ is much worse than θ̂.
likelihood_ratio.example_use: The likelihood ratio test statistic is -2 log Λ, which follows a chi-square distribution asymptotically under H₀.

method_of_moments.definition: A parameter estimation method that equates population moments (functions of θ) to sample moments and solves for θ.
method_of_moments.formula: Set E[Xᵏ] = (1/n)Σ Xᵢᵏ and solve for θ
method_of_moments.intuition: Method of moments says: find the parameter that makes the theoretical mean/variance match what you observed.
method_of_moments.common_confusion: Method of moments estimates are often less efficient than MLE. They are simpler to compute but can be inconsistent if moments don't uniquely identify the distribution.
method_of_moments.example_use: For a Gamma(α, β) distribution, setting theoretical mean and variance equal to sample mean and variance gives MOM estimates.

least_squares_estimation.definition: A parameter estimation method that minimizes the sum of squared differences between observed values and model predictions.
least_squares_estimation.intuition: Least squares finds the parameters that make the model's predictions as close as possible to the data, in a squared-error sense.
least_squares_estimation.common_confusion: OLS is the MLE when errors are normal. For non-normal errors, least squares may not be the most efficient estimator.
least_squares_estimation.example_use: Fitting a line to data by minimizing the sum of squared vertical distances from the line.

bootstrap.definition: A resampling method that approximates the sampling distribution of a statistic by repeatedly resampling with replacement from the observed data.
bootstrap.intuition: The bootstrap simulates the act of taking many samples from the population by treating the observed data as a proxy for the population.
bootstrap.common_confusion: The bootstrap cannot overcome small sample bias or fix a fundamentally flawed study design — it only approximates sampling variability.
bootstrap.example_use: Use 1000 bootstrap samples to construct a confidence interval for the median when the analytical formula is unknown.

non_parametric_bootstrap.definition: The standard bootstrap that resamples with replacement directly from the observed data, making no assumptions about the population distribution.
non_parametric_bootstrap.intuition: The non-parametric bootstrap is assumption-free — it lets the data speak for itself without imposing a distributional model.
non_parametric_bootstrap.example_use: Bootstrap the correlation coefficient between two variables to get a confidence interval without assuming bivariate normality.

parametric_bootstrap.definition: A bootstrap that resamples from a fitted parametric model rather than from the raw data, assuming the model is correctly specified.
parametric_bootstrap.intuition: The parametric bootstrap uses the fitted model to simulate data — it can be more efficient than non-parametric if the model is correct.
parametric_bootstrap.common_confusion: If the parametric model is misspecified, the parametric bootstrap can give misleading results.
parametric_bootstrap.example_use: Fit a normal distribution to data, then generate bootstrap samples from N(X̄, S²) to assess estimator variability.

bootstrap_percentile_method.definition: A bootstrap confidence interval formed by taking the α/2 and 1-α/2 quantiles of the bootstrap distribution of the statistic.
bootstrap_percentile_method.intuition: Cut off the extreme bootstrap values and use the middle 95% as your confidence interval.
bootstrap_percentile_method.common_confusion: The percentile method can have poor coverage if the statistic's distribution is skewed. The BCa (bias-corrected accelerated) method improves on this.
bootstrap_percentile_method.example_use: Sort 1000 bootstrap estimates and take the 25th and 975th values as the 95% CI bounds.
